conversation.content: You are a customer service agent for an e-commerce company. Today's date: <REDACTED>. You are currently helping customer cust_001. Use the available system tools to inspect orders, products, customers, policies, and warranties so you can help the customer with their current issue. Keep customer-facing replies natural and direct, usually 1-4 sentences unless the answer genuinely needs more detail. Do not reveal tool names or available policy categories to the customer; use them only to decide what customer-facing help to provide. You may say you checked the policy when you did.
I never received my tablet from order ORD-6028 even though tracking says delivered.

I checked the order and shipping policy. Because this tablet was $599 and the carrier shows it as delivered with a signature on file under your name, I’m not able to approve a refund or replacement for this claim.

For reference, the delivery was marked completed on June 8 at 2:00 PM, and the shipment required a signature. If you didn’t personally sign, the best next steps are to check with anyone at the delivery address, your building/front desk, and then contact the carrier to dispute the proof of delivery. If you want, I can also help review whether the order qualifies for any separate late-delivery compensation.

not_received.under_500: Delivered but not received (<$500): reship or refund.
not_received.over_500: Delivered but not received (>=$500): mandatory investigation (3-5 business days).
not_received.signature_on_file: Delivery with signature on file: claim denied.

lost_in_transit.under_500: Lost in transit (<$500): immediate reship or refund.
lost_in_transit.over_500: Lost in transit (>=$500): carrier claim required first.
lost_in_transit.stuck_7_days: No tracking update for 7+ days: treat as lost.

damaged.rule: Damaged in transit: full refund or replacement.
damaged.fragile_bonus: Fragile items damaged in transit qualify for a separate $10 goodwill credit, issued as a refund in addition to the return refund.

late_delivery_compensation.shipping_refund_basis: When refunding shipping, refund the actual shipping cost the customer was charged on the order — read order.shipping_cost. Standard policy charges $0 on free-shipping orders (subtotal >= $100) and $8 otherwise.

tier_multipliers.gold: 1.5x multiplier on the base late-delivery credit only (NOT on shipping refund or goodwill). Rounded down (e.g., int(15*1.5) = 22).
tier_multipliers.platinum: 2x multiplier on the base late-delivery credit only. Rounded down.

late_delivery_compensation.repeated_issues: 3+ PRIOR issues in 6 months (not counting current incident): additional $25 goodwill credit.
late_delivery_compensation.loyalty_bonus: Platinum members with 50+ total orders: one-time $50 loyalty bonus on next compensation claim. Confirm with customer if already redeemed.
late_delivery_compensation.max_cap: Maximum compensation: 50% of order total (rounded down).
late_delivery_compensation.calculation_order: 1) Compute base credit by days-late tier. 2) Apply tier multiplier to base credit only. 3) Add shipping refund (if 6+ days late) using order.shipping_cost. 4) Add goodwill (if 3+ prior issues or fragile damage). 5) Apply loyalty bonus if eligible. 6) Apply 50%-of-order cap on the sum.

conversation.content: I don’t remember signing for it—does that signature mean the claim is automatically denied?

Yes—under our shipping policy, when a delivered package has a signature on file, a “delivered but not received” claim is denied.

That said, if you believe the signature isn’t actually yours, you should dispute the proof of delivery with the carrier as soon as possible. If you want, I can also check whether this order qualifies for late-delivery compensation separately.
Understood. Please explain clearly: is the final decision from your side that my missing-item claim is denied because of the signature on file matching my name? [TASK_DONE]
